title: How Trauma Disrupts the Body's Internal Clock
researcherTopic: structural dissociation, healing the fragmented self
metaDescription: Trauma can disrupt the body's internal clock, leaving you feeling stuck in the past. Learn how to gently recalibrate your nervous system and return to the flow of time.
imageDescription: A soft, golden light illuminates a series of interlocking, transparent gears, some of which are subtly misaligned. The overall feeling is one of gentle mechanics, warmth, and the potential for realignment and healing. The background is a soft, out-of-focus warmth, suggesting a return to harmony.
htmlContent:
Janina Fisher, a important voice in understanding the legacy of trauma, has spent decades mapping the terrain of the fragmented self, revealing how overwhelming experiences don’t just leave psychological scars but at its core reorganize our inner architecture. This isn’t merely about bad memories; it’s about a deep disruption that can unmoor us from the natural rhythms of life, including the very cadence of time itself. When the body’s clock is shattered by trauma, we can find ourselves living in a perpetual yesterday, where the past isn’t just a memory but a recurring, visceral reality that dictates the texture of our present moment. The nervous system, in its attempt to keep us safe, can get stuck in a loop, replaying threats that have long since passed, making it feel as though time has folded in on itself, leaving us stranded outside its normal flow.

The Echo of a Disrupted Now

For many who have navigated the labyrinth of complex trauma, the experience of time is not a steady, linear progression but a fractured and unpredictable field. It is a phenomenon where the past bleeds into the present with such intensity that the 'now' becomes a ghost, haunted by the echoes of what was. We might find ourselves reacting to a subtle shift in someone's tone or a fleeting expression with a level of activation that feels entirely disproportionate, only to realize later that the reaction was not to the present event but to a deeply ingrained memory held in the body. In my years of working in this territory, I've seen countless individuals describe this feeling of being perpetually out of sync with their own lives, as if they are watching a film of their experiences rather than living them directly. The body, in its deep and sometimes painful wisdom, remembers the threat so vividly that it refuses to believe the danger has passed, creating a temporal dissonance that can be both disorienting and deeply isolating.

When the Body’s Clock Runs on Survival Time

The human organism is a masterpiece of rhythmic intelligence, a symphony of biological clocks that govern everything from our sleep-wake cycles to our metabolic processes. These internal chronometers are designed to be exquisitely sensitive to the environment, synchronizing our inner world with the outer world’s cycles of light and dark, activity and rest. Trauma, however, works like a seismic shock to this delicate system, forcing it to abandon its natural cadence and adopt a new, more urgent one: survival time. When the nervous system is chronically activated by threat, it shifts its resources away from the long-term, restorative functions of the body and toward the immediate, life-preserving imperatives of fight, flight, or freeze. The result is a body that is perpetually running on emergency fuel, a state of being that is both exhausting and unsustainable. We may find ourselves unable to sleep, even when we are bone-tired, or unable to properly digest our food, even when we are hungry. These are not signs of a broken body but of a body that is intelligently, if painfully, adapted to a world that it no longer trusts to be safe. It’s a state that many find themselves in for years, even decades, before they find their way to resources like the Internal Family Systems Institute, which offers a map to understanding these internal conflicts.

The Unreliable Narrator of Felt Time

One of the most disorienting consequences of a trauma-disrupted internal clock is the deep unreliability of our own felt sense of time. We learn that the clock on the wall and the calendar on our desk are telling one story, while the sensations and activations in our body are telling another, far more urgent one. An afternoon can stretch into an eternity when we are caught in the grip of an emotional flashback, that state where a present-day trigger plunges us back into the emotional reality of a past event without the accompanying visual or auditory memory. That becomes not a failure of memory or a lack of discipline; it is the logical outcome of a system that has been rewired to perceive the world through the lens of threat. The body becomes the primary arbiter of our temporal experience, its sensations of dread, panic, or numbness overriding the cognitive mind’s attempts to stay anchored in the present. A client once described this as living in a house where all the clocks are broken, each one showing a different time, and the only way to know what’s real is to listen to the creaks in the floorboards.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

Re-calibrating the Inner Chronometer

This process of re-calibration is often a dance between the past and the present, a delicate negotiation between the part of us that is still living in survival time and the part that is yearning to return to the flow of life. We may find that as we begin to feel safer in the present, the past comes rushing in with a new intensity, as if the body is finally daring to thaw what has been frozen for so long. What we find here is not a sign that we are going backward, but that we are finally developing the capacity to be with what was once unbearable. We learn to create small islands of presence in the midst of the storm, moments where we can anchor ourselves in the sensory reality of the now - the feeling of our feet on the ground, the warmth of the sun on our skin, the sound of our own breath. These moments may be fleeting at first, but with practice, they begin to string together, creating a new kind of temporal territory, one that is less defined by the echoes of the past and more by the possibilities of the present. It is a process that asks us to reconsider our relationship with healing itself, to move beyond the idea of a cure and into the much more intimate and ongoing practice of befriending ourselves exactly as we are.

Returning to the River of Time

The return to a more regulated experience of time is not a destination we arrive at, but a river we learn to flow with again. It is a gradual and often imperceptible process, one that is marked less by dramatic breakthroughs and more by a quiet accumulation of moments of presence. We may one day notice that we are able to enjoy a simple pleasure ~ a cup of tea, a conversation with a friend, a walk in the woods ~ without the familiar undercurrent of anxiety that has been our constant companion for so long. These are the subtle and deep markers of a nervous system that is beginning to trust the present moment, a body that is slowly learning that the war is over. It is a homecoming that is earned not through striving or effort, but through the patient and persistent practice of doing the work of ourselves, right here, right now, with whatever is true in this moment.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link).

What we find here is not the end of something. This is the beginning of a conversation between you and your own nervous system that most people never have the courage to start. The question is whether you will stay in the room when it gets hard.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so exhausted all the time, even when I get enough sleep?

It turns out to be a hallmark of a nervous system running on survival time. When the body is chronically braced for threat, it diverts enormous amounts of energy to fuel this state of hypervigilance, leaving little left for restorative processes like deep sleep and digestion. Even if you are in bed for eight hours, your system may not be entering the deep, restful states it needs to truly recover, creating a genuine sense of bone-deep weariness that sleep alone cannot seem to touch.

Is it possible to ever feel normal again after complex trauma?

The goal is not a return to a pre-trauma 'normal,' as that state no longer exists, but an integration of the experience into a new, more resilient wholeness. Healing is less about erasing the past and more about expanding your capacity to be present with all parts of yourself, creating a life that feels authentic and aligned with your values now. It is a work of becoming, where the scars of the past become integrated into a map of striking self-knowledge and compassion, a process you can explore further in understanding how the body holds onto these experiences.

A quality yoga mat for body-based practices is Yoga Mat - Manduka PRO (paid link).

Here is the thing, though.

And so we learn, slowly and with great patience, to stop treating our own experience as a problem to be solved. We begin to see that the fractured sense of time, the hypervigilance, the exhaustion - these are not flaws in our character but the echoes of a striking intelligence that knew how to keep us alive. It is in this witnessing that the shattered pieces of our own timeline begin to reassemble, not back into what they were, but into something new, something whole, something uniquely and resiliently our own.

A simple tool for working with specific tension points is Lacrosse Ball for Trigger Points (paid link).

The real question is what you do with this now.
